coredata.dc:title: Ln(III) chelates-functionalized carbon quantum dots: Synthesis, optical studies and multimodal bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Fengshou
Yue, Liangliang
Yang, Lixia
Wang, Kai
Liu, Genyan
Luo, Xiaogang
Zhu, Xunjin
coredata.dc:description: The carbon quantum dots termined with amine groups (CQDs-NH2) were synthesized in one-pot hydrothermal method with citric acid as carbon source and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) as passive agent, which was then covalently linked to 1, 4, 7, 10-tetraazacyclononane (DOTA) framework to yield CQDs-DOTA. The CQDs-DOTA provide excellent ligand scaffolds for the chelation of lanthanide ions, yielding the related complexes CQDs-DOTA-Ln (Ln = Eu, Tb, Yb and Gd). The successful preparation of CQDs-DOTA-Ln was validated by TEM, XRD, XPS, and FT-IR spectroscopy. The CQDs-DOTA-Ln (Ln = Eu, Tb and Yb) exhibited the characteristic emissions of related lanthanide ions, indicating the CQDs could sensitize the luminescence of lanthanide ions. Besides, the CQDs-DOTA-Gd could serve as an excellent T1-weighted MR imaging probe due to the chelation of paramagnetic Gd (III) ions and good hydrophilicity. The cytotoxicity of CQDs-DOTA-Ln was evaluated through MTT assay upon HeLa cells. The images from fluorescence microscopy further verified their applications in bioimaging in vitro. Due to the good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high contrast efficiency, the CQDs-DOTA-Ln with Vis/NIR fluorescence and MR multi-modal imaging performance could be used as potential contrast agents for clinic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Lanthanide
Energy transfer
MRI
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (left) and corresponding size distribution histograms (right) of (A) CQDs-DOTA-Eu, (B) CQDs-DOTA-Tb, (C) CQDs-DOTA-Gd, and (D) CQDs-DOTA-Yb.
XPS spectra of CQDs-DOTA-Eu (A–C), CQDs-DOTA-Tb (D–F), CQDs-DOTA-Yb (G–I), and CQDs-DOTA-Gd (J–L).
(A) XRD patterns, (B) FTIR spectra and (C) Absorption spectra of CQDs-DOTA-Eu, CQDs-DOTA-Tb, CQDs-DOTA-Yb, and CQDs-DOTA-Gd.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) CQDs-DOTA-Eu, (B) CQDs-DOTA-Tb, (C) CQDs-DOTA-Gd, and (D) CQDs-DOTA-Yb with different excitation wavelengths, (E) NIR emission (excited at 400 nm) and related excitation spectra of CQDs-DOTA-Yb, and (F) fluorescence decays (355 nm laser excitation, monitored through a 450 nm bandpass filter) of CQDs-DOTA-Eu, CQDs-DOTA-Tb, CQDs-DOTA-Gd, and CQDs-DOTA-Yb.
The schematic of the energy transfer process of CQDs-DOTA-Ln (Ln = Eu, Tb, Yb).
A) In vitro T1-weighted MRI of CQDs-DOTA-Gd aqueous solutions at varied concentrations, and B) corresponding T1-weighted MRI signal intensity versus concentration of CQDs-DOTA-Gd aqueous solutions.
Cell viability of HeLa cells after incubation with different concentrations (0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 250, and 500 μg/mL) of CQDs-DOTA-Eu, CQDs-DOTA-Tb, CQDs-DOTA-Yb and CQDs-DOTA-Gd for 24 h.

The confocal fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells treated with 250 μg/mL of CQDs-DOTA-Eu (A–C), CQDs-DOTA-Tb (D–F), CQDs-DOTA-Yb (G–I), and CQDs-DOTA-Gd (J–L) for 24 h. Images were taken under a bright field (A, D, G and J), 405 nm excitation (B, E, H and K), and 488 nm excitation (C, F, I and L) (scale bar = 20 μm).

head.ce:title: Ln(III) chelates-functionalized carbon quantum dots: Synthesis, optical studies and multimodal bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of the Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430205, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of the Ministry of Education
School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis and Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, Waterloo Road, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis and Department of Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon quantum dots termined with amine groups (CQDs-NH2) were synthesized in one-pot hydrothermal method with citric acid as carbon source and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI) as passive agent, which was then covalently linked to 1, 4, 7, 10-tetraazacyclononane (DOTA) framework to yield CQDs-DOTA. The CQDs-DOTA provide excellent ligand scaffolds for the chelation of lanthanide ions, yielding the related complexes CQDs-DOTA-Ln (Ln = Eu, Tb, Yb and Gd). The successful preparation of CQDs-DOTA-Ln was validated by TEM, XRD, XPS, and FT-IR spectroscopy. The CQDs-DOTA-Ln (Ln = Eu, Tb and Yb) exhibited the characteristic emissions of related lanthanide ions, indicating the CQDs could sensitize the luminescence of lanthanide ions. Besides, the CQDs-DOTA-Gd could serve as an excellent T1-weighted MR imaging probe due to the chelation of paramagnetic Gd (III) ions and good hydrophilicity. The cytotoxicity of CQDs-DOTA-Ln was evaluated through MTT assay upon HeLa cells. The images from fluorescence microscopy further verified their applications in bioimaging in vitro. Due to the good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high contrast efficiency, the CQDs-DOTA-Ln with Vis/NIR fluorescence and MR multi-modal imaging performance could be used as potential contrast agents for clinic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The lanthanide ions have gained increasing interest for a broad range of applications, such as photovoltaics, bioanalysis, and medical diagnosis, due to their remarkable intrinsic merits in terms of large Stokes shifts, high photostability, and narrow emission band [1–3]. Generally, the luminescence from Ln(III) ions originates from 4f-4f transitions, and the emission bands are very narrow in appearance that could range from UV/visible to NIR regions [4,5]. However, the direct excitation of Ln(III) ions is inefficient since the intraconfigurational 4f-4f transitions are formally forbidden [6]. Thus, a chromophore (antennae) is always needed to sensitize their luminescence through absorbing the light and then transferring the energy to the 4f excited state of Ln(III) ions [4]. As to Eu(III) and Tb(III) ions, the suitable antennas are organic chromophores that could be excited by light between 350–410 nm. For the Ln(III) ions with NIR emission (Yb and Nd), the antennas are usually wider, such as the organic molecules with large π-systems and transition metal complex [7,8]. However, the synthesis of these organic chromophores usually requires cumbersome synthetic steps and harsh synthetic conditions [9]. Recently, the use of nanoparticles to sensitize lanthanide ions has attracted considerable attention because the preparation of nanoparticles is simpler, and their band gap can be tailored by size control, thus improving the efficiency of energy transfer. In addition, the surface of nanoparticles is easier to modify, thus having more potential applications [10]. For example, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have been used as sensitizers for the luminescence of lanthanide ions, due to their unique photophysical properties and good photostability comparing with organic chromophores [11,12]. However, the biomedical application of traditional QDs is always limited as they contained toxic heavy metal elements, such as Pb, Cd, and so on [13].

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great attention due to their remarkable optical properties, low cost and environment-friendly features [14]. Compared with traditional QDs, the CQDs are free of heavy metals, thus displaying better biocompatibility and lower toxicity [15–17]. Based on these merits [18], the CQDs could be used in the fields of bioimaging [19,20], medical diagnosis [21,22], catalysis [23], and photovoltaics [24]. Besides, the CQDs display size-dependent and excitation-dependent PL properties [25], which might be used as suitable sensitizer for the luminescence of Ln(III) ions [26].

Herein, three kinds of lanthanide complexes were covalently conjugated to the surface of carbon quantum dots, as shown in Scheme 1 . The results showed that the CQDs could be used as sensitizer for the luminescence of Ln(III) ions (Eu, Tb, Yb) due to their intense UV absorption band. Consequently, the carbon quantum dots functionalized with lanthanide complexes exhibit the characteristic emissions of related lanthanide ions. Due to the presence of paramagnetic Gd(III) ions, the CQDs-DOTA-Gd exhibited the good T1-weighted MR performance. The cytotoxicity of CQDs-DOTA-Ln upon cancer cells was evaluated through MTT method and they can be used as efficient nanoprobes for multicolor bioimaging.
The synthetic procedure of DOTA-NHS was shown in Scheme 1.

To a 500 mL three-necked flask, 0.4 g (2.3 mmol) 1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane, 1.44 g (2.4 mmol) K2CO3, and 250 mL chloroform were added; 1.5 mL (10.1 mmol) tert-butyl bromoacetate in 100 mL chloroform was then added dropwise at room temperature in a period of 4 h under nitrogen. After stirring for another 3 days, the reaction mixture was filtered to remove the insoluble salts, and the remaining solution was evaporated in vacuo to get the crude product, which was further purified through silica column chromatography, yielding a white solid (0.55 g, 30%).1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3): δ 1.44 (36H, s, -C(CH3)3-), 2.16–2.97 (16H, m, -NCH2CH2N-), 3.04–3.65 (12H, m, -NCH2CO2).

0.300 g (0.45 mmol) tBu-DOTA was dissolved in 6 mL TFA and 6 mL DCM. The residue was stirred at room temperature for 12 h, and then the solvent was concentrated in vacuo. This was followed by the addition of concentration of DCM and diethyl ether yielding a hydroscopic white solid (0.246 g, 91%). 1H NMR (400 MHz, D2O): δ 3.22 (16H, m, -NCH2CH2N-), 4.06 (8H, m, -NCH2CO2-).

DOTA (8.5 mg, 0.021 mmol) was dissolved in H2O (8 mL) at pH 8, to which EDC (8.5 mg, 0.0472 mmol) and sulfonyl NHS (6.72 mg, 0.0304 mmol) were added sequentially at 0 °C. This solution was stirred for 1 h and then stored in the freezer for use in next step.
The synthetic procedure of CQDs-DOTA-Ln was shown in Scheme 2 .

To a 25 mL beaker, 0.6 g BPEI, 1.2 g citric acid, and 10 mL DI water were added. After ultrasonic mixing for 0.5 h, the transparent solution was then heated at 180 °C. When the solvent was evaporated to form a pale-yellow gel, 1 mL DI water was added. This procedure was repeated for several times in a period of 3 h. Finally, the residue was collected and then purified through silica gel column chromatography to yield the desired product.

To a 25 mL single-necked flask, 5 mg (0.013 mmol) DOTA-NHS, 5 mL DI water, and 25 mg CQDs dissolved in 5 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4) were added. The mixture was kept stirring at room temperature for 12 h to obtain CQDs-DOTA. After stirring for 12 h, 0.1 mmol LnCl3 (Ln = Eu, Tb, Yb, Gd) was added dropwise and the resultant solution was stirred for another 3 h. The crude residue was dialyzed against DI water for three days to remove small molecules, which was then freeze-dried to yield pure CQDs-DOTA-Ln.

The synthetic procedures of lanthanide complexes-conjugated carbon quantum dots CQDs-DOTA-Ln were shown in Schemes 1 and 2. Firstly, the CQDs-NH2 were prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid and BPEI according to the previous literature [27]. The 1, 4, 7, 10-tetraazacyclododecane-1, 4, 7, 10-tetraacetic acid (DOTA) was synthesized through the reported method [28]. The carboxyl group of DOTA was selectively activated through a standard EDC-NHS reaction at stoichiometric ratio, which was then reacted with amine groups on the surface of CQDs-NH2, forming an amide bond. LnCl3 was chelated with the proligand (CQDs-DOTA) in H2O at pH 5.5 to give CQDs-DOTA-Ln. The aqueous solution of CQDs-DOTA-Ln did not show any precipitation or aggregation and any change of UV–vis absorbance after storage at ambient condition for more than two months, suggesting the good dispersion stability of the as-prepared nanodots.

The TEM images in Fig. 1 confirmed the quantum size of the as-prepared materials. All the five materials are spherical and well dispersed. As shown in Fig. S1, the average size of CQDs-DOTA is about 2.8 nm, which was slightly smaller than that of CQDs-DOTA-Eu (3.0 nm, Fig. 1A), CQDs-DOTA-Tb (3.3 nm, Fig. 1B), CQDs-DOTA-Gd (3.4 nm, Fig. 1C), and CQDs-DOTA-Yb (3.5 nm, Fig. 1D).

The survey XPS spectrum of CQDs-DOTA and Fig. S2 A in Fig. 2 A indicated that the composites contain carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements [29]. A high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S2B) showed three peaks at 283.8, 285.4, and 287.9 eV, which could be assigned to CC/CC, C–N/CO, and CON. The N 1s spectrum of CQDs-DOTA (Fig. S2C) was resolved into two peaks at 398.9 and 403.2 eV, corresponding to the C-N-C and N-C, respectively. In addition, the XPS spectrum of CQDs-DOTA-Eu (Fig. 2A-C), CQDs-DOTA-Tb (Fig. 2D-F), CQDs-DOTA-Yb (Fig. 2G-I) and CQDs-DOTA-Gd (Fig. 2J-L) showed the peaks of Eu 4d, Tb 4d, Yb 4d and Gd 3d, respectively, which further confirms the successful chelation of Ln(III) ions on CQDs-DOTA. The high resolution XPS spectrum of Eu 4d (Fig. 2C), Tb 4d (Fig. 2F), Yb 4d (Fig. 2I) and Gd 3d (Fig. 2L) were located around 145.3, 155.9, and 187.5 eV, and 1189.5 eV, respectively [30,31].

The typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of all five as-prepared materials in Fig. 3 S and Fig. 3A displayed only one broad peak at around 22°, which is consistent with those reported carbon quantum dots, indicating the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials [18].

The FT-IR spectroscopy was also carried out to characterize the functional groups of CQDs-DOTA and CQDs-DOTA-Ln. As shown in Fig S3 and Fig. 3B, the peak at 3437 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH; the peaks at 1625 and 1390 cm−1 are attributed to the CC and CN stretching vibration, respectively. The peaks around 2935 and 1722 are assigned to the CH and CO stretching, respectively [27].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs-DOTA and CQDs-DOTA-Ln displays two peaks at 250 and 365 nm, respectively as shown in Fig S4A and Fig. 3C, attributed to the π–π* and σ–π* transition [32]. The aqueous solution of five materials all exhibited strong blue light upon UV irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 A-D, the maximum emission peak of as-prepared materials was located 450 nm when excited at 300 nm. Moreover, the CQDs-DOTA and CQDs-DOTA-Ln exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, with the fluorescence peak shifting from 450 to 500 nm upon 240–440 nm excitation, probably due to the different surface energy trap distribution of CQDs. Moreover, the chelation of lanthanide did not significantly shift the maximum peak of CQDs-DOTA (Fig. S4B).

Upon photo excitation between 240–340 nm, the CQDs-DOTA-Eu showed significant characteristic emissions of Eu3+ ion (insert in Fig. 4A) due to transitions from 5D0 to the ground multiple 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4); the peak at 578 nm was ascribed to 5D0→7F0; the peak between 588 and 595 nm was assigned to 5D0 →7F1; the peaks at 613, 651, and 678 nm were attributed to 5D0 →7F2, 5D0→7F3, and 5D0 →7F4, respectively [10]. The CQDs-DOTA-Tb also exhibited the characteristic emissions of Tb3+ ion (insert in Fig. 4B) from 5D4 to the ground multiple 7FJ (J = 6, 5, 4, 3) [31]. The strongest emission (λem = 541 nm) and the peak at 485 nm were ascribed to the 5D4→7F5 and 5D4→7F6 transition, respectively. Moreover, the CQDs-DOTA-Yb displayed the characteristic emission of Yb3+ ions in the near infrared region (Fig. 4E), with maximum peak at about 980 nm, corresponding to the 2F5/2→2F7/2 transition of Yb3+ [26]. The related excitation spectra of CQDs-DOTA-Yb measured at 980 nm was centered at around 400 nm. Remarkably, the emission spectrum (λem = 450 nm) of CQDs-DOTA-Yb in visible region (Fig. 4D) was partly overlapped with the excitation spectrum of lanthanide ions (Fig. 4E). Thus, the characteristic emission peak of CQDs-DOTA-Yb in the NIR region could be assigned to the energy transfer (ET) from CQDs to Yb3+ ions. Accordingly, the luminescence intensity of CQDs-DOTA-Yb in the visible region decreased to some extent in comparison with mere CQDs-DOTA, mainly ascribed to ET of the excited state of CQDs (donor) to the Yb3+ ion center (acceptor). Under the excitation of 360 nm, the FL quantum yield of CQDs-DOTA-Eu, CQDs-DOTA-Tb, and CQDs-DOTA-Yb was calculated to be 10.1%, 14.8% and 15.4%, respectively, lower than CQDs-DOTA (25.3%) without Ln3+ chelation, suggesting the existence of energy transfer between CQDs-DOTA and lanthanide ions.

To verify the energy transfer between CQDs and lanthanide ions, the fluorescence decay curve was recorded through a time-correlated single photon counting method. As shown in Fig. 4F, the average lifetimes of CQDs-DOTA-Eu, CQDs-DOTA-Tb and CQDs-DOTA-Yb are determined to be 5.34, 7.12 and 9.17 ns, respectively, which is shorter than that of CQDs-DOTA without lanthanides (15.86 ns), indicating energy transfer from CQDs-DOTA to lanthanide ions. The schematic of the energy transfer process between carbon quantum dots and lanthanide ions was shown in Fig. 5 .

Due to the good hydrophilicity and the successful chelation of paramagnetic Gd (III) ions on the surface of CQDs-DOTA, the CQDs-DOTA-Gd exhibited the good T1-weighted MR performance. The MR contrast properties of CQDs-DOTA-Gd were measured at different concentrations of 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 6 , T1-weighted images of CQDs-DOTA-Gd displayed greater brightness with the increase of concentration, indicating it could be served as a good positive contrast agent.

In addition, the cytotoxicity of CQDs-DOTA-Ln against HeLa cells was carried out through MTT assay. The cell viability as a function of CQDs-DOTA-Ln concentrations (0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 250 and 500 μg/mL) was measured. As depicted in Fig. 7 , all four CQDs-DOTA-Ln exhibited very low cytotoxicity, i.e., viability over 85% even at a concentration of 500 μg/mL after 24 h’ incubation, indicating the lanthanide ions were not released in cells. These results suggested that the CQDs-DOTA-Ln have good biocompatibility with cancer cells, which might be suitable for further biomedical applications.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of CQDs-DOTA-Ln for bioimaging applications, we used a confocal fluorescence microscope to observe the distribution of CQDs-DOTA-Ln in HeLa cells. The HeLa cells labeled with CQDs-DOTA-Ln (250 μg/mL) displayed blue (Fig. 8 B, E, H and K) and green (Fig. 8C, F, I and L) fluorescence under 405 nm and 488 nm light excitation, respectively, consistent with the excitation-dependent FL behavior of CQDs-DOTA-Ln. Besides, all four CQDs-DOTA-Ln were distributed mainly over the cell cytoplasm, and the nucleus had very weak fluorescence, which was similar with those CQDs reported before [17,33].

In summary, four new nanoprobes were facilely prepared through surface conjugation of CQDs-NH2 with DOTA and further Ln3+ chelation. The microstructure and chemical compositions of the as-prepared nanoprobes CQD-DOTA-Ln (Ln = Eu, Tb, Yb, and Gd) were characterized in detail through various methods. The as-prepared CQDs displayed good water solubility and high fluorescence quantum yield. Impressively, the CQDs-DOTA-Ln emitted the characteristic fluorescence of related lanthanide ions, indicating the efficient energy transfer between CQDs-DOTA and lanthanide ions (Eu, Tb and Yb). Besides, the CQDs-DOTA-Gd could serve as an excellent T1-weighted magnetic resonance imaging probe due to the chelation of paramagnetic Gd (III) ions. The biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of CQDs-DOTA-Ln upon HeLa cells were further evaluated through MTT assay. In addition, these nanoprobes could be used for cellular multicolor imaging, indicating their great potential for Vis/NIR fluorescence and MR multi-modal imaging and biomedical applications in vivo.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21601142) and Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2017CFB689). X. Zhu thanks Hong Kong Research Grants Council (HKBU 22304115-ECS) and the financial support from Hong Kong Baptist University (RC-IRMS/16/17/02CHEM, RC-ICRS/15-16/02E and FRG2/14-15/034).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.11.054.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
